analogy: Think of "Era of One Party Dominance" through the chapter's own opening images: Credit: Shankar In this chapter… The challenge of nation-building, covered in the last chapter, was accompanied by the challenge of instituting democratic politics. Thus, electoral competition among political parties began immediately after Independence. In this chapter, we look at the first decade of electoral politics... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 26 November 1949 and signed on 24 January 1950 and it came into, 25 November 1949, 28 Politics in India sinc Independence, 489 Members of Lok Sabha. Only 15 per cent of these eligible voters. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Era of One Party Dominance is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that credit: Shankar In this chapter… The challenge of nation-building, covered in the last chapter, was accompanied by the challenge of instituting democratic politics.

The text explains that thus, electoral competition among political parties began immediately after Independence.

The text explains that in this chapter, we look at the first decade of electoral politics in order to understand • the establishment of a system of free and fair elections; • the domination of the Congress party in the years immediately after Independence; and • the emergence of opposition parties and their policies.

The text explains that this famous sketch by Shankar appeared on the cover of his collection – Don’t Spare Me, Shankar.

The text explains that the original sketch was drawn in the context of India’s China policy.

The text explains that but this cartoon captures the dual role of the Congress during the era of one-party dominance.

The text explains that challenge of building democracy You now have an idea of the difficult circumstances in which independent India was born.

The text explains that you have read about the serious challenge of nation-building that confronted the country right in the beginning.

The text explains that faced with such serious challenges, leaders in many other countries of the world decided that their country could not afford to have democracy.

The text explains that they said that national unity was their first priority and that democracy will introduce differences and conflicts.

The text explains that therefore many of the countries that gained freedom from colonialism experienced non-democratic rule.

The text explains that it took various forms: nominal democracy but effective control by one leader, one party rule or direct army rule.

The text explains that non-democratic regimes always started with a promise of restoring democracy very soon.

The text explains that but once they established themselves, it was very difficult to dislodge them.

The text explains that the conditions in India were not very different.

The text explains that but the leaders of the newly independent India decided to take the more difficult path.

The text explains that every society needs to decide how it will govern and regulate itself.

The text explains that democratic politics is an answer to this question.

The text explains that last year you studied how our Constitution was drafted.

The text explains that you would remember that the Constitution was adopted on 26 November 1949 and signed on 24 January 1950 and it came into effect on 26 January 1950.

The text explains that at that time the country was being ruled by an interim government.

The text explains that it was now necessary to install the first democratically elected government of the country.

The text explains that the Constitution had laid down the rules, now the machine had to be put in place.

The text explains that ambedkar Speech in Constituent Assembly 25 November 1949 “ “ 2chapter era of one-party dominance

The text explains that by that time many countries in Europe had not given voting rights to all women.

The text explains that an Indian editor called it “the biggest gamble in history”.

The text explains that the Hindustan Times claimed that “there is universal agreement that the Indian people have conducted themselves admirably in the largest experiment in democratic elections in the history of the world”.

The text explains that india’s general election of 1952 became a landmark in the history of democracy all over the world.

The text explains that in which States, did the other parties perform reasonably well?

The text explains that the Congress scored big victory in those elections as well.

The text explains that it won a majority of seats in all the states except Travancore-Cochin (part of today’s Kerala), Madras and Orissa.

The text explains that finally even in these states the Congress formed the government.

The text explains that the most significant of these cases was in Kerala in 1957 when a coalition led by the CPI formed the government.

The text explains that apart from exceptions like this, the Congress controlled the national and all the state governments.

The text explains that the extent of the victory of the Congress was artificially boosted by our electoral system.

The text explains that for this, you need to recall the discussion about the first-past- the-post method in your textbook, Indian Constitution at Work last year.

The text explains that in this system of election, that has been adopted in our country, the party that gets more votes than others tends to get much more than its proportional share.

The text explains that for the first time in the world, a Communist party government had come to power through democratic elections.

The text explains that on losing power in the State, the Congress party began a ‘liberation struggle’ against the elected government.

The text explains that in 1959 the Congress government at the Centre dismissed the Communist government in Kerala under Article 356 of the Constitution.

The text explains that this decision proved very controversial and was widely cited as one of the prominent instances of the misuse of constitutional emergency powers.

The text explains that namboodiripad, leading a procession of Communist Party workers, after his ministry was dismissed from office in Trivandrum in August 1959.

The text explains that in 1948, the Congress amended its constitution to prevent its members from having a dual party membership.

The text explains that but the socialists faced a dilemma when in 1955 the Congress declared its goal to be the socialist pattern of society.

The text explains that in some countries like China, Cuba and Syria the constitution permits only a single party to rule the country.

The text explains that does it mean that we have had a coalition government since 1952? had already started its campaign.

The text explains that congress as social and ideological coalition You have already studied the history of how Congress evolved from its origins in 1885 as a pressure group for the newly educated, professional and commercial classes to a mass movement in the twentieth century.

The text explains that this laid the basis for its eventual transformation into a mass political party and its subsequent domination of the political system.

The text explains that take a political map of India (with State outlines) and mark: (a) two states where Congress was not in power at some point during 1952-67.

The text explains that (b) two states where the Congress remained in power through this period.

The text explains that read the following passage and answer the questions below: “Patel, the organisational man of the Congress, wanted to purge the Congress of other political groups and sought to make of it a cohesive and disciplined political party.

The text explains that he …. sought to take the Congress away from its all-embracing character and turn it into a close-knit party of disciplined cadres.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Politics in India since Independence, Chapter 2: Era of One Party Dominance

prelims_traps: Do not treat 26 November 1949 and signed on 24 January 1950 and it came into and 25 November 1949 as identical unless the source text links them directly.

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

options: Non-democratic regimes always started with a promise of restoring democracy very soon.
But once they established themselves, it was very difficult to dislodge them.
But the leaders of the newly independent India decided to take the more difficult path.
Any other path would have been surprising, for our freedom struggle was deeply committed to the idea of democracy.

Every society needs to decide how it will govern and regulate itself.
There are always different policy alternatives to choose from.
